title: Multiple Letters at Once (Block Substitution / MNV)
learning_goal: Learn that a real mutational event can replace SEVERAL CONSECUTIVE nucleotides at the same time in a single codon - a multi-nucleotide variant (MNV) or 'block substitution' - rather than just one letter (a SNP, as in Levels 2-3). This is a real, if less common, class of variant seen in real sequencing data, and it usually has a bigger effect on the amino acid than a single-letter change because more of the codon's identity changes at once.
rules: You'll work with whichever gene you chose in Level 1 - your choice carries over automatically.
This sample has TWO CONSECUTIVE nucleotides changed together, in the same codon, as a single block substitution - not one isolated letter like Levels 2-3.
Click each of the 2 changed nucleotides in the sample row (in either order) to find the whole block before moving on.
Both letters belong to the SAME codon, so there is only one codon - and one amino acid - to re-translate afterward.
Changing 2 letters of a codon at once usually changes the amino acid, since far fewer codon pairs happen to share an amino acid than single-letter neighbors do.
materials_provided: The reference (known/healthy) nucleotide sequence and full amino acid sequence, shown on screen the whole time
Your sample's full nucleotide sequence, shown alongside the reference so you can click to locate BOTH changed nucleotides in the block
An amino acid picker (all 20 amino acids plus the '*' STOP symbol) to fill in the blanked codon once you've found the whole block
instructions: 1. Compare the reference DNA row against your sample DNA row and click BOTH nucleotides that differ - they sit right next to each other, in the same codon.
2. Once both are found, that codon is revealed as a blank in your protein row. Look up the changed codon in the codon table and click the amino acid you think it now codes for.
3. Compare your completed protein row against the reference protein row.
4. The reveal explains the block substitution: which two letters changed together, and whether the resulting amino acid is the same or different.

block_substitution_details.change: CT -> AG at positions 1-2 of codon 7 (both letters changed together, in the same mutational event)
block_substitution_details.classification: Non-synonymous (missense) block substitution - CTC (Leucine) becomes AGC (Serine), so the protein changes at position 7.

block_substitution_details.change: CC -> AG at positions 1-2 of codon 7 (both letters changed together, in the same mutational event)
block_substitution_details.classification: Non-synonymous (missense) block substitution - CCA (Proline) becomes AGA (Arginine), so the protein changes at position 7.

block_substitution_details.change: AT -> GC at positions 1-2 of codon 7 (both letters changed together, in the same mutational event)
block_substitution_details.classification: Non-synonymous (missense) block substitution - ATA (Isoleucine) becomes GCA (Alanine), so the protein changes at position 7.
